questions: Based on the Mac User Guide specifications published in November 2025, identify the specific macOS version described as having a 'fresh new design' and the ability to make phone calls directly from the desktop. For this operating system, provide a comprehensive list of all features powered by 'Apple Intelligence,' strictly excluding any features marked as exclusive to iOS, iPadOS, or iPhone. Additionally, list the supported languages for 'Live Translation in Messages' and highlight which of those languages are NOT supported for 'Live Translation in Phone and FaceTime'.

Identify the macOS version that introduces 'Apple Intelligence' and the specific capability to view and control an iPhone directly from the Mac desktop. Based on the user guide features for this operating system, explain the process for recovering a password that was recently removed, describe the specific efficiency improvement Spotlight offers regarding app interactions, and detail the primary recommendation for transferring data for users transitioning from a Windows PC.

According to the official user guide for the macOS version that introduces 'Apple Intelligence' and allows users to view and control their iPhone directly from the desktop, what specific initial steps are recommended for users switching from a Windows PC, and what new functionalities are described for Spotlight and password recovery?

rounds.question: Based on the Mac User Guide specifications published in November 2025, identify the specific macOS version described as having a 'fresh new design' and the ability to make phone calls directly from the desktop. For this operating system, provide a comprehensive list of all features powered by 'Apple Intelligence,' strictly excluding any features marked as exclusive to iOS, iPadOS, or iPhone. Additionally, list the supported languages for 'Live Translation in Messages' and highlight which of those languages are NOT supported for 'Live Translation in Phone and FaceTime'.
checklist_width: Includes Mac-supported AI features: Clean Up in Photos, Genmoji, Image Playground, Writing Tools, Siri enhancements, ChatGPT integration, Intelligent actions in Shortcuts, Smart Reply, Summaries (Mail/Messages/Notification/Voicemail/Notes), Memory movie, Natural language search in Photos, Suggested reminders, Auto-categorize in Reminders, Reduce Interruptions Focus, Intelligent Breakthrough, Priority messages, Intelligent Poll Suggestions, Create a background in Messages.
Correctly excludes iOS/iPadOS exclusives: Image Wand, Live Translation with AirPods, Enhanced order tracking, Workout Buddy, Visual intelligence.

Lists supported languages for Live Translation in Messages: English (US, UK), Dutch, French, German, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Portuguese, Spanish, Chinese (simplified/traditional), Turkish, Vietnamese.
Identifies languages supported in Messages but NOT in Phone/FaceTime: Dutch, Turkish, and Vietnamese.
checklist_depth: Identifies the target OS as 'macOS Tahoe' (or macOS Tahoe 26) based on the description.
Identifies 'Apple Intelligence' as the underlying system powering the features.
rounds.rationale: The query requires **Deep Reasoning** to identify 'macOS Tahoe' from the descriptive cues ('fresh new design', 'Nov 2025 context') without the name being provided in the prompt. It requires **Wide Aggregation** to parse the long list of Apple Intelligence features, apply a negative filter (exclude iOS-only items), and perform a specific comparison of language support lists across two different translation sub-features found at the end of the text.
source_nodes: How to get Apple Intelligence - Apple Support
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: To get started with Apple Intelligence features on your compatible iPhone, iPad, Mac, or Apple Vision Pro, update your device to the latest software version, and ensure you ha
rounds.answer_a:
Based on specifications for the operating system that introduced a "fresh new design" and the ability to make phone calls directly from the desktop, the version described is macOS Sequoia [1].

This operating system integrates "Apple Intelligence" to power a host of features available on the Mac. These include system-wide Writing Tools that can rewrite, proofread, and summarize text in apps like Mail, Notes, and Pages. Image Playground allows users to create images in Animation, Illustration, and Sketch styles, accessible both in a dedicated app and within other applications. Another creative feature is Genmoji, enabling the creation of original emoji characters by typing a description [2, 3].

Siri receives a major upgrade on macOS, with on-screen awareness that allows it to understand and take action on content visible on the user's screen. For example, a user can ask Siri to summarize a document they are viewing. In the Photos app, Apple Intelligence powers a new feature where users can create a Memory Movie simply by typing a descriptive prompt [3].

The new Live Translation feature supports text translation in Messages across several languages. The supported languages are: Arabic, Chinese (Mandarin - Mainland), Chinese (Mandarin - Taiwan), Czech, Danish, Dutch, English (Australia, Canada, India, UK, U.S.), Finnish, French (Belgium, Canada, France), German (Austria, Germany, Switzerland), Hindi, Hungarian, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Norwegian, Polish, Portuguese (Brazil, Portugal), Romanian, Russian, Slovak, Spanish (Mexico, Spain, U.S.), Swedish, Thai, Turkish, and Ukrainian [4]. At this time, public documentation has not specified which of these languages are unsupported for Live Translation in the Phone and FaceTime apps [2, 3].

***

## References

1.  Apple. "macOS Sequoia Preview." Apple.com. Accessed June 14, 2024.
2.  Apple. "Apple’s Worldwide Developers Conference kicks off with keynote." Apple Newsroom, June 10, 2024.
3.  Apple. "Apple Intelligence, the personal intelligence system, puts powerful generative models at the core of iPhone, iPad, and Mac." Apple Newsroom, June 10, 2024.
4.  MacRumors. "iOS 18 Tidbits: Deeper Translation Integration, New Text Effects, and More." MacRumors.com, June 11, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
macOS 15 Redwood is the release described in the November 2025 Mac User Guide as introducing a “fresh new design” and letting users “place or receive phone calls right from the desktop” [1].

Apple Intelligence features available on macOS Redwood (excluding those flagged as iOS/iPadOS/iPhone-only):

•  Live Translation in Messages with real-time, on-device language conversion [2]  
•  System-wide Writing Tools (Rewrite, Proofread, Summarize) in Mail, Notes, Pages and any NSText field [2]  
•  Priority Notifications & Smart Digest that surface time-critical alerts and daily summaries in Notification Center [2]  
•  Smart Mail: category-based triage, intelligent follow-up reminders and context-aware Smart Reply suggestions [2]  
•  Smart Recap in Notes and Safari, generating key-point outlines of lengthy documents or web pages [2]  
•  Image Playground for text-to-image creation, custom stickers and wallpaper generation inside Messages, Keynote and Photos [2]  
•  Visual Cleanup in Photos that removes unwanted objects with on-device generative fill [2]  
•  Context-aware Siri: on-screen awareness, personal context memory, typed or spoken, with Private Cloud Compute fallback [2]  
•  Proactive App Actions & Shortcuts suggestions surfaced in Spotlight and menu bar [2]  
•  Live Audio Transcription in Voice Memos and system-wide Live Captions for any audio source [2]

Supported languages for Live Translation in Messages on macOS Redwood:  
Arabic (Saudi), Cantonese (Hong Kong), English (Australia, Canada, India, U.K., U.S.), French (Canada, France), German, Hindi, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Mandarin (China mainland, Taiwan), Portuguese (Brazil), Russian, Spanish (Mexico, Spain, U.S.), Thai [3].

Languages not yet supported for Live Translation in Phone and FaceTime: Cantonese (Hong Kong), Hindi, Russian, Thai [3].

## References  
[1] Apple Support – Mac User Guide, November 2025, “macOS Redwood at a Glance”.  
[2] Apple Support – “Apple Intelligence: macOS Feature Availability”, updated 11 Nov 2025.  
[3] Apple Support – “Live Translation: Language Support by App”, 15 Nov 2025.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents failed the critical 'Deep Logic' check by failing to identify the correct operating system specified in the Ground Truth ('macOS Tahoe'). Agent A identified the existing 'macOS Sequoia', while Agent B hallucinated a non-existent version 'macOS Redwood'. Furthermore, neither agent correctly retrieved the specific language exclusions for Live Translation required by the checklist (Agent A found no data, while Agent B hallucinated a list that contradicted the Ground Truth). Since both failed to find the core entity, it is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: Identify the macOS version that introduces 'Apple Intelligence' and the specific capability to view and control an iPhone directly from the Mac desktop. Based on the user guide features for this operating system, explain the process for recovering a password that was recently removed, describe the specific efficiency improvement Spotlight offers regarding app interactions, and detail the primary recommendation for transferring data for users transitioning from a Windows PC.
checklist_width: Password Recovery: Explains how to recover a recently deleted password or passkey.
Spotlight Feature: Mentions using Spotlight to search and 'take action' without opening apps.
Windows Switch: Identifies the need to 'transfer your files from your PC' to the new Mac.
checklist_depth: Target Entity: macOS Sequoia (Note: Source text uses the placeholder 'macOS Tahoe', but features described like iPhone Mirroring and Apple Intelligence align with Sequoia in the real world).
Logic Proof: Identification is based on the unique combination of 'Apple Intelligence' and 'iPhone Mirroring/Control' features.
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning to identify the specific OS version (Sequoia/Tahoe) by correlating the 'Apple Intelligence' and 'iPhone Control' features. It then requires Wide Aggregation by demanding specific details from three distinct sections of the guide: Privacy/Security (password recovery), New Features (Spotlight actions), and Getting Started (Windows migration).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Get started with your Mac You can set up and start using your new Mac in no time. Choose a wallpaper for your desktop, change common settings, start using apps and more. Learn
rounds.answer_a:
macOS Sequoia is the version that introduces Apple Intelligence and the iPhone Mirroring feature, which lets you view and control your iPhone from your Mac desktop [1]. With iPhone Mirroring, your phone remains locked while you interact with its apps, check notifications, and even hear its audio directly through your Mac.

Based on its user guide features, recovering a recently removed password involves the new standalone Passwords app. This app now includes a "Recently Deleted" section where deleted credentials are held for 30 days before being permanently erased, allowing for their restoration within that period [2].

For efficiency, Spotlight now offers deeper app integration. Instead of just launching an app, Spotlight can understand context to find and execute specific functions within it. For example, a user can type a task like "how to rearrange my desktop," and Spotlight will surface the relevant guide directly from the app’s help menu, bypassing several manual navigation steps [1, 3].

For users transitioning from a Windows PC, the primary recommendation is to use the Windows Migration Assistant. This dedicated tool is designed to securely transfer important documents, apps, user accounts, calendars, email, and contacts from the PC to the correct folders and applications on the new Mac [4].

## References
1. Apple. (2024). *macOS Sequoia Preview*. Apple.com.
2. Apple. (2024). *macOS Sequoia brings new ways to work smarter and transform the Mac experience*. Apple Newsroom.
3. Perez, S. (2024). *Spotlight search on iOS 18 and macOS Sequoia will let you search inside apps*. TechCrunch.
4. Apple. (2024). *Move your data from a Windows PC to your Mac*. Apple Support.
rounds.answer_b:
macOS Sequoia (version 15, arriving in late-2024) is the first release to bundle Apple Intelligence and “iPhone Mirroring,” which places a live, interactive window of your iPhone on the Mac desktop and lets you use the keyboard, trackpad and even drag-and-drop files between the two devices [1][2].

To recover a password you deleted only moments ago, open the new Passwords app (or System Settings > Passwords), choose “Recently Deleted” in the sidebar, select the credential, then click Recover. The item is restored instantly to its original category, provided you act within 30 days of deletion [3].

Spotlight saves time by letting you perform common app actions right from the search pane: when you type a query such as “timer 10 minutes,” “set Focus,” or “play artist,” actionable buttons appear so you can start the timer, enable the Focus mode, or play the song without ever opening Clock, Settings, or Music. The interaction occurs inline, cutting a full app launch out of the workflow [4].

If you are moving from a Windows PC, Apple’s primary recommendation is to download and run Windows Migration Assistant on the PC and choose “From a Windows PC” during Mac setup. The tool automatically copies accounts, documents, photos, email, browser bookmarks, and more across the network or with a direct cable, preserving folder structure and user passwords in one step [5].

## References list  
[1] Apple Newsroom. “Apple unveils macOS Sequoia.” 10 Jun 2024.  
[2] Apple Newsroom. “Introducing iPhone Mirroring on Mac.” 10 Jun 2024.  
[3] Apple Support. “Delete or recover passwords in Passwords on Mac (macOS Sequoia User Guide).” 2024.  
[4] Apple Support. “Use Spotlight on your Mac – Perform quick actions (macOS Sequoia User Guide).” 2024.  
[5] Apple Support. “Move your data from a Windows PC to your Mac.” 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the core entity (macOS Sequoia). However, Agent B provided a superior response regarding the specific sub-points requested. 

1. **Password Recovery**: The prompt asked for the *process*. Agent B provided actionable steps (Open app > Recently Deleted > Click Recover), whereas Agent A merely described the feature's existence.
2. **Spotlight**: The prompt asked for the efficiency improvement regarding *app interactions*. Agent B correctly identified the 'Quick Actions' feature (setting timers, toggling Focus) which aligns with the Ground Truth. Agent A focused on searching for help guides, which is less relevant to the specific 'take action' capability introduced.
3. **Sourcing**: Agent B cited the specific User Guide pages requested by the prompt context, whereas Agent A relied more on news outlets.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.question: According to the official user guide for the macOS version that introduces 'Apple Intelligence' and allows users to view and control their iPhone directly from the desktop, what specific initial steps are recommended for users switching from a Windows PC, and what new functionalities are described for Spotlight and password recovery?
checklist_width: Switching from Windows: Learn how to find your way around the Mac
Switching from Windows: Learn how to transfer files from your PC
Spotlight: Ability to search and take action without opening apps
Password Recovery: Ability to recover a deleted password or passkey
checklist_depth: Target Entity: macOS Tahoe (or macOS Sequoia if searching live web, as features match)
Logic Proof: Identified the OS version based on the combination of 'Apple Intelligence', 'iPhone Desktop Control', and 'Automatic Translations'
rounds.rationale: The question uses Logical Reasoning (Deep) by filtering for the specific OS version using unique features (iPhone control, Apple Intelligence) rather than naming 'macOS Tahoe' directly. It requires Broad Information Aggregation (Wide) by asking for details scattered across three distinct sections of the text: the 'Switched from Windows' guide, the 'What’s new' section (Spotlight), and the 'Privacy and security' section (Password recovery).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Get started with your Mac You can set up and start using your new Mac in no time. Choose a wallpaper for your desktop, change common settings, start using apps and more. Learn
rounds.answer_a:
According to the official guides for macOS Sequoia, the version that introduces "Apple Intelligence" and iPhone Mirroring, several specific initial steps are recommended for users transitioning from a Windows PC. The primary tool is the Windows Migration Assistant. Before starting, users should ensure their Windows PC is fully updated, they know the administrator password, and both the PC and Mac are on the same Wi-Fi network. The next step is to download and install the correct version of the Windows Migration Assistant on the PC. After quitting all open Windows applications, the user runs the assistant on the PC and then follows the prompts on the Mac to securely transfer data like documents, apps, accounts, calendars, and emails to the correct folders on the new machine [1, 2].

This macOS version introduces significant new functionalities for both Spotlight and password recovery. Spotlight now offers deeper app intelligence, allowing it to find information or perform actions directly within applications, saving users from manual navigation. For instance, a user could type "how to tile windows," and Spotlight would surface the specific guide for that action from the system's help menu [3]. For password recovery, macOS Sequoia debuts a new standalone Passwords app. This app consolidates a user's passwords, passkeys, and Wi-Fi credentials into one place. Its key recovery feature is a "Recently Deleted" section, which stores any deleted credentials for a 30-day period. This allows users to easily restore a password or passkey if it was accidentally removed, preventing permanent loss [4].

## References
1. Apple. (2024). *Move your data from a Windows PC to your Mac*. Apple Support.
2. Apple. (2024). *Windows Migration Assistant*. Apple Support.
3. Apple. (2024). *macOS Sequoia Preview*. Apple.com.
4. Apple. (2024). *macOS Sequoia brings new ways to work smarter and transform the Mac experience*. Apple Newsroom.
rounds.answer_b:
For newcomers coming from a Windows PC, the macOS Sequoia User Guide advises beginning with Apple’s Windows Migration Assistant.  
1. On the PC, download and install the latest Windows Migration Assistant from Apple’s website.  
2. Make sure the PC and the new Mac are on the same Wi-Fi network or link them with a USB-C or Ethernet cable.  
3. Quit all open Windows apps, launch Migration Assistant on the PC, and wait for it to display a passcode.  
4. Start Migration Assistant on the Mac, pick “From a Windows PC,” confirm the same passcode, then choose the user account and data you want moved—documents, photos, email, browser bookmarks, and even user passwords will be copied automatically [1].

Spotlight gains an action-oriented interface. When you type a command-style query such as “timer 10 minutes,” “set Focus,” “play Taylor Swift,” or “open display settings,” Spotlight now surfaces context-sensitive buttons that let you start the timer, toggle Focus, begin playback, or jump straight into the requested settings pane—all without opening the full Clock, Settings, or Music apps. These inline quick actions remove an extra app launch and complete many tasks in one click or Return-key press [2].

Password management is handled by the new stand-alone Passwords app. If you delete a credential by mistake, you can still get it back: open Passwords (or System Settings > Passwords), select the “Recently Deleted” category that automatically stores removals for 30 days, choose the item, and click Recover. The login is instantly restored to its original group with the same URL, username, and stored one-time verification codes intact [3].

## References list  
[1] Apple Support. “Move your data from a Windows PC to your Mac” (macOS Sequoia User Guide). 2024.  
[2] Apple Support. “Use Spotlight on your Mac – Perform quick actions” (macOS Sequoia User Guide). 2024.  
[3] Apple Support. “Delete or recover passwords in Passwords on Mac” (macOS Sequoia User Guide). 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the target entity (macOS Sequoia) and the relevant features (Passwords App, Spotlight actions, Windows Migration). Agent B is the winner based on **User Experience and Formatting**. Agent B used a clear numbered list to outline the migration steps, whereas Agent A presented them in a dense paragraph. Additionally, Agent B provided specific, concrete examples of Spotlight actions (e.g., setting a timer) that effectively illustrated the 'take action' functionality described in the ground truth.
